sevenDayForecast.headline: Tropical Cyclone Koji near the northeast Queensland coast. After weakening post-landfall, likely to move south of the Gulf of Carpentaria then further west in the longer term.
points: Forecast Track Maps and Tropical Cyclone Advices are being issued for Tropical Cyclone Koji (12U) and should be referred to for more information. A Severe Weather Warning and Flood Watches and Warnings are also current.
Koji will remain a cyclone until landfall this morning.

After landfall ex-TC Koji is forecast to move to the west and then northwest, south of the Gulf of Carpentaria. There is a Very Low risk of redevelopment over the south of the Gulf of Carpentaria next week.
The remnants of the system may then continue further west towards northern WA next weekend.

trackForecast.headline: Tropical Cyclone Koji (12U) lies east southeast of Innisfail, with impacts increasing overnight ahead of coastal crossing during Sunday morning.
trackForecast.discussion:
Tropical Cyclone Koji (12U) lies over the Coral Sea east southeast of Innisfail as a broad monsoonal system with winds peaking just into category 2. Koji is moving south towards the northeast Queensland coast, and likely to cross the coast between Townsville and Bowen during Sunday morning.

Observations at offshore reefs and Willis Island confirm gales in at least three quadrants. Koji is forecast to remain a category 2 system until it interacts with land during Sunday morning.

The Abbott Point area is currently under a Tropical Cyclone Warning. Gales with damaging wind gusts to 100 km/h are likely tonight, with the potential for destructive wind gusts during Sunday morning, particularly if 12U tracks further south than the current forecast. Heavy to intense rainfall is also possible in the area tonight and on Sunday.

After crossing the coast, the winds of the system will quickly weaken and the risk of gales should rapidly ease. Heavy rain may continue through Sunday and Monday.
trackForecast.discussionUpperBound: The upper bound is based on the system slowly developing until landfall, rather than maintaining intensity as is currently forecast, and being slower to reach the coast thus having more time to develop.
